programs.name: Wyoming Medicaid Long-Term Care
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home care or daily help at home, Wyoming Medicaid can cover the full cost of care.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Wyoming Medicaid Long-Term Care covers the full cost of nursing home care or in-home support services. This isn't regular health insurance—it's comprehensive care coverage for seniors who meet strict income and asset limits.

Wyoming offers three types of coverage: Nursing Home Medicaid (pays for full nursing home care), HCBS Waivers (pays for in-home personal care, meal delivery, adult day care, and home modifications to help your parent stay home), and ABD Medicaid (basic healthcare plus individualized long-term care services). Your parent must be 65+ with income under $2,982/month and assets under $2,000 for individuals.

Because Wyoming uses an income cap system, if your parent's income exceeds $2,982/month, they can still qualify by setting up a Qualified Income Trust (Miller Trust)—but this requires specific legal planning many families miss.

countedAssets: Real estate other than primary home
exemptAssets: One vehicle for medical transport or employment

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must demonstrate Nursing Facility Level of Care (clinical assessment showing your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Wyoming residency
U.S. citizenship or 5+ years as lawful permanent resident
Medical assessment using LT101 screening tool

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your county public health office, which handles the required medical assessment.
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent's income exceeds $2,982/month, ask about setting up a Qualified Income Trust before applying—this legal tool can make them eligible.

steps.title: Contact your county public health office
steps.description: Each county handles medical assessments using the LT101 screening tool to evaluate your parent's need for daily care assistance.

steps.title: Complete the LT101 medical assessment
steps.description: A public health nurse will assess your parent's ability to perform activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, and taking medications.

steps.title: Submit financial documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of all income sources (Social Security, pensions, wages, rental income) and asset documentation (bank accounts, investments, property).

steps.title: Choose your care option
steps.description: If approved, select from Nursing Home Medicaid, HCBS Waivers for in-home care, or ABD Medicaid for individualized services.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home
Cash value of life insurance policies
excluded: Primary home (if equity under $602,000 and your parent intends to return or spouse/disabled child lives there)
One vehicle used for medical transport or employment

categories.name: Financial
items: Records of any asset transfers in past 5 years

contentSections.text: Wyoming enforces a 60-month look-back period for asset transfers. If your parent gifted money or sold assets below market value in the past 5 years, it could create a penalty period that delays coverage.

If your parent's income is over $2,982/month, don't assume they're ineligible. A Qualified Income Trust (Miller Trust) can redirect excess income to meet the limit—but this requires legal setup before applying.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Wyoming Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the home equity is under $602,000 and she intends to return home, or if her spouse or a disabled/minor child lives there. The house won't count toward the $2,000 asset limit.

faqs.question: What if Dad's Social Security and pension total $3,200/month—over the $2,982 limit?
faqs.answer: He can still qualify by setting up a Qualified Income Trust (Miller Trust) that redirects the excess $218/month to meet the income limit. This must be established before applying and requires legal documentation.

faqs.question: What's the difference between HCBS Waivers and Nursing Home Medicaid?
faqs.answer: HCBS Waivers pay for in-home personal care, meal delivery, adult day care, and home modifications so your parent can stay home. Nursing Home Medicaid covers full nursing facility care. Both require the same medical assessment showing need for daily care assistance.

faqs.question: How long does the medical assessment take?
faqs.answer: Your county public health office conducts the LT101 assessment, which evaluates your parent's ability to perform daily activities like bathing and dressing. Contact your local office for current scheduling—processing times aren't specified in state guidance.

faqs.question: Will ABD Medicaid cover all the services Mom needs right away?
faqs.answer: No. ABD Medicaid benefits are evaluated individually and provided one service at a time, unlike Nursing Home Medicaid which covers comprehensive care. Each service request requires separate approval based on medical necessity.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom gave money to grandchildren last year?
faqs.answer: Any gifts or below-market-value asset transfers in the past 60 months could create a penalty period that delays Medicaid coverage. The penalty length depends on the total amount transferred divided by Wyoming's average monthly nursing home cost.

programs.name: Community Choices Waiver (CCW)
programs.tagline: Helps your parent stay home instead of moving to a nursing home by covering personal care, homemaker services, and adult day programs.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, Wyoming's Community Choices Waiver covers personal care services, homemaker help, adult day programs, and respite care. The program serves people 65+ or those 19-64 with disabilities who qualify for Medicaid and need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.

The waiver has a waiting list because Wyoming caps enrollment at approximately 3,669 people statewide. Your parent must first qualify for Medicaid (income up to about $2,901/month for individuals) and pass a nursing facility level of care assessment. Priority goes by the date you apply, so applying early matters even if your parent isn't ready for services yet.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment using the LT-101 tool that evaluates if your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Wyoming resident and U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Must be eligible for Wyoming Medicaid (income up to approximately $2,901/month for individuals)
Annual reassessment required to maintain services

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your county Benefits & Eligibility Specialist to start the process — they'll help with both Medicaid and CCW applications.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid first
steps.description: Your parent must be Medicaid-eligible before applying for CCW. If not already enrolled, apply through your county specialist or online.

steps.title: Submit CCW application
steps.description: Complete CCW Form 01 (Program Application and Fact Sheet) with your county Benefits & Eligibility Specialist.

steps.title: Schedule nursing assessment
steps.description: A Public Health Nurse will use the LT-101 assessment tool to determine if your parent meets nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Select case management agency
steps.description: Choose from approved agencies in your county — they'll coordinate all services once approved.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time not specified in official sources
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes — not an entitlement program. Capped at approximately 3,669 slots statewide with priority by eligibility determination date.
applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if your parent isn't ready for services yet — your place on the waiting list is determined by application date.

categories.name: Medical
items: Nursing facility discharge date if applicable

contentSections.text: There is a waiting list. Wyoming caps enrollment at about 3,669 people, and priority goes by application date. Apply early even if your parent isn't ready for services.
You must qualify for Medicaid first. If your parent's income or assets are over Medicaid limits, they cannot get CCW services. Apply for or renew Medicaid before starting the CCW application.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for CCW?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary residence is typically exempt from Medicaid asset limits, which CCW follows. Your parent can receive CCW services while living in their own home — that's the whole point of the program.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: They cannot qualify for CCW. The program requires Medicaid eligibility first, with income limits around $2,901/month for individuals. There may be special Medicaid waiver groups with higher limits — ask your county specialist about options.

faqs.question: How long is the waiting list for CCW services?
faqs.answer: Wyoming doesn't publish current wait times. Priority goes by your application date, so apply as soon as your parent meets the requirements, even if they don't need services immediately.

faqs.question: What's the difference between participant-directed and agency-directed care?
faqs.answer: Participant-directed means your parent (or you as their representative) hires and manages their own caregivers. Agency-directed means a home care agency provides and manages the workers. You choose the model that works best for your family.

faqs.question: Can my parent get CCW services in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes. CCW allows your parent to live at home or in assisted living instead of a nursing facility. The services can follow them to different residential settings.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent no longer needs nursing home-level care?
faqs.answer: They'll lose CCW eligibility. The program requires annual reassessment using the LT-101 tool. If your parent's care needs decrease below nursing facility level, they'll be discharged from CCW.

programs.name: PACE Wyoming
programs.tagline: All-in-one medical care for parents who need nursing home level care but want to stay home — but Wyoming's program closed in 2021.

localEntities.name: Formerly WY PACE at Cheyenne Regional Medical Center (closed 2021)

programs.intro:
PACE would have provided complete medical care under one roof for your parent — primary care, specialists, medications, transportation, adult day care, and home services all coordinated by one team. If your parent was 55+ and needed nursing home level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management), they could have stayed home with PACE managing everything.

Wyoming's only PACE program closed in October 2021 due to state budget cuts. The program operated only in the Cheyenne area through Cheyenne Regional Medical Center. For dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients, PACE was free with no co-pays or deductibles.

While PACE is unavailable in Wyoming, you can explore other home and community-based services through Wyoming Medicaid, or consider PACE programs in neighboring states if your parent is willing to relocate.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Live in the service area of a PACE organization
Able to live safely in the community with PACE support

applicationGuide.summary: PACE Wyoming is no longer available — the program closed in 2021.

steps.title: Contact Wyoming Medicaid
steps.description: Call Wyoming Department of Health to explore alternative home and community-based services

steps.title: Consider neighboring states
steps.description: If your parent is open to relocating, research PACE programs in Colorado, Montana, or other nearby states

steps.title: Explore other options
steps.description: Ask about Wyoming's other Medicaid waiver programs for home care services

applicationGuide.tip: Focus on Wyoming's other Medicaid home and community-based waiver programs as alternatives.

contentSections.text: Wyoming's only PACE program closed in October 2021 due to state budget cuts. If you're researching PACE for your parent, you'll need to look into alternative Wyoming Medicaid programs or consider PACE programs in neighboring states.

Even though PACE is unavailable, Wyoming offers other Medicaid waiver programs for home and community-based services. Contact Wyoming Medicaid to explore what's available for your parent's care needs.

faqs.question: Why did Wyoming's PACE program close?
faqs.answer: Wyoming's PACE program closed in October 2021 due to state budget cuts. The state could not continue funding the program.

faqs.question: Were there other PACE programs in Wyoming besides Cheyenne?
faqs.answer: No. Wyoming had only one PACE program, operated through Cheyenne Regional Medical Center in the Cheyenne area.

faqs.question: Can my parent access PACE in a neighboring state?
faqs.answer: Possibly, but your parent would need to move to that state and live in a PACE service area. Each PACE program serves specific geographic regions and requires participants to live within those boundaries.

faqs.question: What alternatives exist in Wyoming for comprehensive care?
faqs.answer: Wyoming offers other Medicaid waiver programs for home and community-based services. Contact Wyoming Department of Health at health.wyo.gov to learn about current options for your parent's care needs.

faqs.question: Could PACE return to Wyoming in the future?
faqs.answer: It's possible but uncertain. PACE programs require significant state investment and federal approval. There's no current timeline for reintroducing PACE in Wyoming.

faqs.question: What was covered when PACE Wyoming was operating?
faqs.answer: PACE covered all medical care, medications, transportation to appointments, adult day health care, home care services, and hospital care with no co-pays or deductibles for participants.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI) in Wyoming
programs.tagline: Help your parent save $600–4,000+ annually on Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments if their income is under $1,816/month.
programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare costs, Wyoming's Medicare Savings Programs can cover their premiums, deductibles, and copayments. These programs work in three tiers: QMB (most comprehensive), SLMB (Part B premiums only), and QI (Part B premiums plus prescription drug help). Your parent must be enrolled in Medicare and meet income limits ranging from $1,350–1,816/month for a single person, depending on which tier they qualify for.

Because these programs are administered through Wyoming's Medicaid system, your parent gets automatic coverage without coordinating between multiple agencies. The QMB program provides the most value — it eliminates nearly all Medicare out-of-pocket costs for people with income under $1,350/month. Asset limits are relatively low at $9,090 for a single person, so families with modest savings should check eligibility carefully.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums ($185/month) plus deductibles and copayments for QMB tier

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A and/or Part B
Must apply annually to maintain benefits
QI Program: Cannot qualify for any other Medicaid coverage

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through Wyoming Department of Health or your county social services office — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Contact Wyoming Medicaid
steps.description: Call Wyoming Department of Health, Division of Healthcare Financing or visit your county Department of Social Services office for application forms.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, and Medicare enrollment. The $20 income disregard per household is automatically applied.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Mail completed application to your state Medicaid agency or apply in person at your local Medicaid office.

steps.title: Reapply annually
steps.description: QI Program operates first-come, first-served, so reapply early each year. Priority given to previous year recipients.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI Program operates on a first-come, first-served basis with priority given to people who received QI benefits in the previous year
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QI Program early in the year since funding may be limited and it operates first-come, first-served.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Part A premiums, Part B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments for all Medicare-covered services

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only (must have both Part A and Part B to qualify)

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.description: Premium help plus prescription drug benefits
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums and automatically qualifies for Extra Help prescription drug program with copayments capped at $12.65 per drug

contentSections.text: QI Program funding is limited and operates first-come, first-served. Apply early each year, especially if your parent received benefits previously.

Income limits include a $20 disregard per household, so your parent can earn $20 more than the stated limits and still qualify. Asset limits are strict — exceeding $9,090 (single) disqualifies regardless of income.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the QMB limit but under the SLMB limit?
faqs.answer: She would qualify for SLMB, which covers Medicare Part B premiums (saving about $2,220/year) but not deductibles or copayments. The programs are designed as tiers — if you don't qualify for one level, you may qualify for the next.

faqs.question: Can Dad keep his house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify which assets are exempt in Wyoming. Typically, a primary residence doesn't count toward the $9,090 asset limit, but you'll need to confirm with Wyoming's Medicaid agency which assets are excluded.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom qualifies for QMB but we miss the annual reapplication?
faqs.answer: She'll lose benefits until you reapply. All three programs require annual reapplication. Set a calendar reminder to reapply early each year, especially for QI Program which operates first-come, first-served.

faqs.question: If Dad has other Medicaid coverage, can he still get QI benefits?
faqs.answer: No. QI Program is only available for people who don't qualify for any other Medicaid coverage or benefits. If he qualifies for QMB or SLMB, he cannot use QI — but those programs provide more comprehensive coverage anyway.

faqs.question: How much will this actually save on Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: QMB provides the most savings — eliminates Part B premiums ($185/month), deductibles, and all copayments for Medicare-covered services. SLMB and QI save $2,220/year on Part B premiums alone. Total savings depend on how much Medicare care your parent uses.

faqs.question: What if we can't find the Wyoming application forms online?
faqs.answer: The state website doesn't provide direct links to application forms. You'll need to contact Wyoming Department of Health, Division of Healthcare Financing directly or visit your county Department of Social Services office to get the specific forms and local contact information.

programs.name: SNAP (Food Stamps)
programs.tagline: Up to $298-$1,789 monthly for groceries if your parent is on a fixed income

localEntities.name: Local county Department of Family Services offices

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford groceries on a fixed income, SNAP (food stamps) provides monthly money loaded onto an EBT card for food purchases. Your parent can receive $298-$1,789 per month depending on household size and income — money that works like a debit card at grocery stores and farmers markets.

SNAP has special rules that favor seniors. If your parent is 60+, there's no gross income limit (only net income after deductions matter), higher asset limits ($4,500 vs $3,000), and unlimited deductions for housing costs. Because these deductions can significantly lower countable income, many seniors qualify even if their initial income seems too high.

In Wyoming, you must apply through your county Department of Family Services office. Processing typically takes 30 days, and if approved, benefits start the month after application.
programs.savingsSource: Maximum monthly benefits: $298 (1 person) to $1,789 (8+ people) × 12 months

otherRequirements: Must apply in county where you live
No work requirements if household is entirely 60+ or disabled

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at dfs.wyo.gov or call your county DFS office — processing takes about 30 days.

steps.title: Find your county office
steps.description: You must apply in the county where your parent lives. Find the local DFS office at dfs.wyo.gov.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect recent pay stubs or Social Security letters, bank statements, rent/mortgage receipts, utility bills, and medical expense receipts if your parent is 60+.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at dfs.wyo.gov, call the county office, or visit in person. Phone and in-person applications may be processed faster.

steps.title: Complete interview
steps.description: A caseworker will schedule a phone interview to review the application and verify information.

applicationGuide.tip: Medical expenses over $35/month can be deducted from income for seniors, so bring all prescription and doctor receipts.

contentSections.heading: Net Income Limits (After Deductions)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits. Seniors 60+ have no gross income limit — only these net limits after deductions.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: SSI recipients (automatically exempt from asset test)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Utility bill or rent receipt showing Wyoming address

categories.name: Income
items: Recent pay stubs if still working

contentSections.text: Seniors 60+ get unlimited deductions for housing costs (rent, mortgage, utilities). If your parent pays more than half their income on housing, this deduction often makes them eligible even with higher incomes.

faqs.question: My mom gets $1,800 from Social Security. Can she still qualify?
faqs.answer: Possibly. Seniors 60+ have no gross income limit — only net income after deductions matters. If she pays high housing costs or medical expenses, deductions could bring her net income below $1,305/month for a single person household.

faqs.question: Will SNAP affect my dad's other benefits like Medicare or Social Security?
faqs.answer: No. SNAP doesn't count as income for other programs and won't reduce Social Security, Medicare, or other benefits. It's designed to supplement, not replace, other assistance.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP benefits to buy groceries online?
faqs.answer: Yes. SNAP EBT cards work for online grocery orders at participating stores like Amazon, Walmart, and many local grocers. This is especially helpful if your parent has mobility issues.

faqs.question: What if my mom lives in Cheyenne but I want to help her apply from Casper?
faqs.answer: She must apply through the Laramie County DFS office where she lives, not where you live. You can help her apply online or call her local office, but the application goes through her county.

faqs.question: My parent has $6,000 in savings. Does this disqualify them?
faqs.answer: If your parent is 60+ or disabled, they can have up to $4,500 in countable assets. However, if they receive SSI, the asset test doesn't apply at all. The $6,000 would disqualify them unless they spend down to $4,500 or qualify for an exemption.

faqs.question: How long do SNAP benefits last?
faqs.answer: There's no time limit if your parent continues to qualify. They'll need to recertify periodically (usually every 12-24 months for seniors), but benefits continue as long as they meet income and asset requirements.

programs.name: Wyoming Energy Assistance (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Help your parent pay heating bills with up to $2,176 toward propane, heating oil, or utilities this winter.
programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay heating bills during Wyoming winters, this program pays money directly to their utility company or fuel vendor. Because Wyoming uses 60% of state median income instead of federal poverty guidelines, more families qualify than in other states — a two-person household earning up to $46,000 annually can receive help.

The program covers primary heating costs like propane, heating oil, wood pellets, coal, and electric or gas utilities. Your parent can receive $49 to $2,176 per heating season depending on household size, income, and heating type. Parents age 60+ get priority processing, and emergency assistance up to $550 is available for shutoff notices or broken heating equipment.

Applications for the 2025-2026 heating season are open through April 30, 2026. Because demand is high statewide, the phone system uses callbacks to hold your place in line rather than keeping you on hold.
programs.savingsSource: One-time seasonal payment directly to utility or fuel vendor

otherRequirements: Must be Wyoming resident
Must be responsible for home energy costs (homeowners, renters, or permanently parked RVs)
Household includes all people at address sharing utility bill

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at lieapwyo.org or call 1-800-246-4221 for callback — payments begin October 1st for priority cases.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online at lieapwyo.org, call 1-800-246-4221 for callback, or contact local Community Action Agency or Area Agency on Aging for phone, mail, or in-person help.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of identity, recent utility bill, income proof for all household members, Social Security numbers, and proof of U.S. citizenship or eligible immigration status.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: NOWCAP contractor determines eligibility. Priority given to households with someone 60+, disabled, or children under 6 until October 1st.

steps.title: Payment processing
steps.description: If approved, payment goes directly to utility company or fuel vendor — not to your parent. Crisis assistance processes faster than regular heating assistance.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Payments start October 1 for crisis cases; others after October 1
applicationGuide.waitlist: Higher-than-normal applications reported; phone callback system manages demand
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SSI or SNAP, they may automatically qualify for energy assistance.

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Social Security numbers for all household members
Proof of U.S. citizenship or eligible immigration status

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of income for all household members
Recent utility bill showing account and balance
Documentation of heating fuel costs if not utility-based

contentSections.text: Applications for regular heating assistance are only accepted October 1 through April 30. Crisis assistance (shutoff notices, broken heating equipment) is available year-round but processes faster after October 1st.
If your parent is 60+, disabled, or has young children in the home, their application gets priority processing. Use the callback phone system (1-800-246-4221) instead of waiting on hold.

faqs.question: What if Mom heats with propane or wood instead of regular utilities?
faqs.answer: The program covers all primary heating fuels including propane, heating oil, wood pellets, coal, and utilities. Payment goes directly to your fuel vendor or utility company, not to your parent.

faqs.question: Can Dad apply if he lives in a mobile home or RV?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as the RV is permanently parked and he's responsible for the energy costs. Homeowners, renters, and RV residents all qualify if they meet income requirements.

faqs.question: What counts as household size for the income limits?
faqs.answer: Everyone living at the address who shares the utility bill, even if they're not related. This is different from SNAP — it's based on who lives there and shares energy costs.

faqs.question: Will this cover Mom's entire heating bill for the winter?
faqs.answer: No, this is partial assistance. The program provides one payment per heating season ranging from $49 to $2,176 depending on household size, income, and heating type. It helps reduce costs but doesn't cover the full bill.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: If he receives SSI or SNAP benefits, he may still automatically qualify regardless of the income limits. Otherwise, he won't qualify for regular assistance but may still be eligible for crisis assistance if there's a shutoff or emergency.

faqs.question: How quickly can Mom get help if her heat gets shut off?
faqs.answer: Crisis assistance up to $550 is available for emergencies like shutoffs or broken heating equipment. These cases process faster than regular applications, especially after October 1st.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home insulation and energy improvements to cut your parent's heating bills by 5–25%.

localEntities.name: Wyoming Department of Family Services (DFS) oversees.

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with high heating bills, Wyoming's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free home improvements that can cut energy costs by 5–25%. Licensed technicians will seal air leaks around doors and windows, add insulation, and perform basic health and safety checks at no cost to your family.

Your parent qualifies if their household income is at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level, or if they already receive SNAP, SSI, TANF, or Wyoming's energy assistance (LIEAP). The program covers single-family homes, apartments, and mobile homes statewide. Because this uses a priority system that favors seniors, disabled adults, and families with young children, older parents often move up the waitlist faster.

summary: Income at or below 200% Federal Poverty Level
Home not for sale within 12 months
otherRequirements: Wyoming resident
Home not expected to be sold or rented within 12 months
Home not previously weatherized by DOE-related program
Renters need written landlord permission

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online year-round at lieapwyo.org or call 800-246-4221 — processing time varies but crisis cases get expedited.

steps.title: Complete joint LIEAP/WAP application
steps.description: Use the combined Wyoming LIEAP, WAP & LIHWAP application form online or by phone. Weatherization is automatically considered when you apply.

steps.title: Submit required documents
steps.description: Provide proof of income and residency. Renters must also submit a completed Rental Verification form or Weatherization Landlord Agreement.

steps.title: Wait for approval and priority assignment
steps.description: Your application is reviewed and assigned priority points. Elderly, disabled, and families with children under 5 get higher priority.

steps.title: Schedule energy audit
steps.description: Once approved and prioritized, a local weatherization agency will contact you to schedule a home energy audit and begin work.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies; crisis applications (e.g., lost heat, low fuel) expedited. Once approved, weatherization agency contacts for energy audit.
applicationGuide.waitlist: Services based on priority point system (elderly, disabled, children under 5 prioritized); approval does not guarantee services same year
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has a heating emergency or is nearly out of fuel, mention this when applying — crisis cases get expedited processing.

contentSections.text: Approval doesn't guarantee weatherization services the same year. The program uses a priority system and has funding limits, so there may be a waitlist even after you're approved.
If your parent rents, get landlord permission in writing before applying. The landlord must agree not to raise rent or evict tenants because of the improvements.

categories.name: Income Verification
items: Proof of income (if not already receiving SNAP, SSI, TANF, or LIEAP)
Most recent pay stubs or Social Security statements

categories.name: Housing Documentation
items: Proof of home ownership or rental agreement

faqs.question: What if my parent's landlord won't give permission?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot receive weatherization services without written landlord permission via the Rental Verification form or Landlord Agreement. Try explaining that the improvements increase property value at no cost to the landlord.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist after approval?
faqs.answer: It varies by funding and demand, but seniors and disabled adults get priority points that move them up faster. The program notes that approval doesn't guarantee services in the same year.

faqs.question: What if their home was weatherized years ago?
faqs.answer: Homes previously weatherized through any DOE-related program are not eligible. However, if the work was done through a utility company or private contractor (not a government program), they may still qualify.

faqs.question: Can mobile home owners get weatherization?
faqs.answer: Yes, the program covers single-family homes, multi-family units, and mobile homes statewide. The same income limits and priority system apply regardless of housing type.

faqs.question: What happens if they need to sell their house soon?
faqs.answer: Homes expected to be sold or rented within 12 months are not eligible. The program requires that your parent will continue living in the home to benefit from the energy savings.

faqs.question: Do they automatically qualify if they get other benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent receives LIEAP (Wyoming's energy assistance), SNAP, SSI, or TANF, they automatically meet the income requirements. You'll still need to submit the application and meet other requirements like residency.

programs.name: Wyoming Home Services Program (WyHS)

stateOverview.intro: Wyoming offers 7 key programs that can save your parent $2,000–10,000+ annually on medical care, groceries, and home energy costs. These range from Medicaid long-term care (covering the full cost of nursing home care or daily help at home) to smaller but immediate help like SNAP food benefits ($298–1,789/month) and energy assistance (up to $2,176 this winter). Most programs have income limits around $1,800–2,200/month for a single person, but some like Weatherization Assistance are available regardless of income. Start by checking if your parent qualifies for Wyoming Medicaid Long-Term Care or Medicare Savings Programs — these typically offer the largest financial impact and can be applied for immediately.

startHere.name: Wyoming Medicaid Long-Term Care
startHere.why: Covers the full cost of nursing home care or daily help at home — potentially worth $50,000+ annually.

startHere.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
startHere.why: Can save $600–4,000+ annually on Medicare costs if income is under $1,816/month.

startHere.name: SNAP (Food Stamps)
startHere.why: Provides immediate monthly grocery assistance ($298–1,789) for parents on fixed incomes.

byNeed.need: Help paying for nursing home or home care
byNeed.description: These programs cover the cost of professional caregiving, whether at home or in a facility.
Help your parent save hundreds or thousands on Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments.
Monthly benefits to help cover grocery costs for parents on limited incomes.

byNeed.need: Help with heating and utility bills
byNeed.description: Direct bill payment assistance plus free home improvements to permanently reduce energy costs.

quickFacts: Most Wyoming benefit programs have income limits between $1,800–2,200/month for a single person
PACE Wyoming closed in 2021, so parents needing all-in-one medical care should explore Community Choices Waiver instead
Wyoming Energy Assistance provides up to $2,176 this winter — applications typically open in October
Weatherization Assistance Program offers free home improvements regardless of income in some cases
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: All 7 programs Wyoming offers are qualification-based benefits that require meeting income or care need requirements. For example, SNAP requires low income, while Community Choices Waiver requires your parent to need nursing home level care. Wyoming doesn't appear to offer universal free resources like information hotlines — most assistance is tied to specific eligibility criteria around income, age, or medical need.
